verses.text: The LORD: these are beasts ye may eat. Whatever parts the hoof and chews the cud — eat. Camel chews but doesn't divide — unclean. Coney and hare chew but don't divide — unclean. Swine divides but doesn't chew — unclean. Touch their carcass — unclean. Guidance: Two markers must agree — split hoof and chewed cud. Neither alone qualifies. God teaches Israel to read the world by two signs at once. Holiness lives where inside and outside line up.

Of those in the waters: whatever has fins and scales in seas and rivers — eat. What has neither fins nor scales — abomination. Their carcasses abomination; eat not their flesh, abhor their carcasses. Guidance: Again two marks must agree — fins and scales. Eels, shrimp, shellfish all fall on the wrong side. God's people learn to look twice at every menu. The lesson is discernment more than dietetics.

Of fowls these are abominable: eagle, ossifrage, ospray, vulture, kite, raven, owl, night hawk, cuckow, hawk, little owl, cormorant, great owl, swan, pelican, gier eagle, stork, heron, lapwing, bat. Guidance: Mostly birds of prey, scavengers, things that eat blood or carrion. God's people stay away from what eats death. The diet teaches identity — Israel will not be carrion-eaters in any sense.

All flying creeping things on four — abomination. Yet of these eat: locust, bald locust, beetle, grasshopper — those with legs above their feet to leap. All others on four — abomination. Guidance: Four legs that crawl — no. Four legs that leap above the ground — yes. The leap makes the difference. John the Baptist ate locusts in the wilderness; he stayed within the law. The lowest food is clean if God names it so.

Touching the carcass of any unclean beast — unclean till evening. Bearing it — wash clothes, unclean till evening. Cattle that part the hoof but not cloven — unclean. Whatever goes on paws — unclean. Carry the carcass — wash clothes, unclean till evening. Guidance: Even handling the dead body of an unclean animal stains till sunset. The day is shaped around purity — wash, wait, let evening clear you. God's people learn to feel the difference between holy and unclean hours.

Creeping things unclean: weasel, mouse, tortoise, ferret, chameleon, lizard, snail, mole. Touching dead — unclean till evening. Whatever they fall on — wood, cloth, skin, sack — wash, clean by evening. Earthen vessel falls into — break. Seed sown clean unless water be on it. Guidance: A dead lizard on a clay pot ruins it. The same lizard on dry seed doesn't ruin the seed. Death travels through liquid more easily than dry. God teaches what carries impurity and what doesn't.

If any beast you may eat dies — touch its carcass, unclean till evening. Eat thereof — wash clothes, unclean till evening. Carry it — wash clothes, unclean till evening. Guidance: Even a clean animal becomes unclean by dying on its own. Death itself is the contagion, not the species. Israel's holiness laws guard the camp against the touch of death more than against any one creature.

Israel's holiness laws guard the camp against the touch of death more than against any one creature.

Every creeping thing on the earth — abomination; not eaten. Whatever goes on the belly, on four feet, has many feet — not eaten. Defile not yourselves with any creeping thing; not make yourselves unclean. Guidance: The list keeps tightening because the temptation is wide. Anything that crawls on its belly carries the curse pronounced on the serpent in Genesis 3. Israel eats nothing that looks like the first liar.

I am the LORD your God; sanctify yourselves and be holy, for I am holy. I brought you out of Egypt; ye shall be holy. This is the law of the beast, fowl, water creature, creeping thing — to distinguish unclean from clean. Guidance: The reason for every food law: God is holy, so His people must be too. Peter cites this in 1 Peter 1. Holiness reaches into the kitchen. What you put in your mouth shapes who you are.
